danger_signals.visual_form: Clay-animated character design with menacing traits (clown, scarecrow-ghost, snowman with 'bad' demeanor)
danger_signals.meaning: Visual parody of fighting game archetypes signals combat readiness and opponent danger through caricature

danger_signals.visual_form: Blob enemy, Octohead, HoboCop, and beast-like wrestlers rendered in clay
danger_signals.meaning: Grotesque or inhuman appearance communicates threat level; larger/buff physiology (Tiny the wrestler) signals grapple danger

danger_signals.visual_form: Ickybod Clay (pumpkin-headed scarecrow ghost) and ghostly character designs
danger_signals.meaning: Otherworldly appearance signals unnatural combat abilities; parodies horror fighting game tropes

safety_signals.visual_form: Clay animation rendering, exaggerated proportions, parody character archetypes (Boogerman, Earthworm Jim cameo)
safety_signals.meaning: Humorous aesthetic de-escalates threat perception; signals game is self-aware parody rather than serious combat simulation

safety_signals.visual_form: Player access to diverse clay-character designs across all game titles
safety_signals.meaning: Multiple selectable character options signal fairness, player agency, and balanced matchup potential

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Appears on all series cover art; consistent recurring antagonist presence

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Clown design (parody of Mortal Kombat Kano/Street Fighter Bison archetypes)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Playable in 4 of 5 games; non-playable once (C2: Judgment Clay)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Scarecrow-ghost with pumpkin head; supernatural clay constructs
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Appears in 4 games; thematic consistency across titles

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Franchise-specific visual identities imported from Interplay properties
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Limited appearance (63⅓ and Sculptor's Cut only); marked as non-native roster

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Clay-rendered character sprites in fighting stance; parody title frames reference fighting game tournament structure (ClayFighter: Tournament Edition)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: One-on-one match format; character movesets reflect fighting game hierarchy (rushdown clown vs. grappler wrestler vs. projectile-user blob)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Exaggerated clay animation style; character designs (Boogerman, Tiny with comically large teeth, Taffy candy fighter) emphasize parody
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game mechanics parody Street Fighter/Mortal Kombat conventions; tone signals humorous combat rather than serious violence

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ickybod Clay scarecrow-ghost with pumpkin head; ghostly character designs increase in prominence across sequels
Tournament Edition and Sculptor's Cut visual presentation indicates special/limited roster versions
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Unique character availability signals content gating; visual differentiation from base ClayFighter implies expanded roster

environmental_storytelling.example: Bad Mr. Frosty's snowman form appearing on all cover art establishes him as series mascot/protagonist; consistency signals narrative continuity across titles
Hoppy character as 'spoof of Terminator' communicates game's meta-commentary on fighting game tropes; visual callbacks to famous archetypes ground worldstate in pop-culture pastiche
Rotation of monster archetypes (clown, scarecrow-ghost, blob, wrestler, candy, rabbit) across sequels tells implicit story of ClayFighter universe as monster-fighting carnival/circus
Earthworm Jim and Boogerman appearances in 63⅓ and Sculptor's Cut indicate Interplay universe convergence; visual presence of non-native characters signals expanded fictional world

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Protagonist archetype appearing on all covers; persistent roster presence signals main character status
Playable in 4 of 5 games, non-playable in C2: Judgment Clay; indicates selective narrative relegation
Appears in 4 games; presence increases emphasis on supernatural worldstate; persistent across sequels signals canonical importance
Only in 63⅓ and Sculptor's Cut; visual import from external franchises signals series expansion and crossover worldstate
Playable in 4 of 5 games; appears in all titles except one, indicating canonical but non-essential status
Only in 3 games; buff physiology and grappler archetype; thematic positioning as strength-based threat indicator

notes: ClayFighter is a pure parody/pastiche fighting game with minimal lore documentation. World-state communication relies entirely on visual character design and clay animation medium. No documented faction politics, diplomatic structures, or complex world-lore exists in available sources. Game communicates primarily through: (1) character archetype parody, (2) clay animation aesthetic as tonal signal (comedy vs. threat), (3) roster composition changes across sequels indicating worldstate shifts. The absence of environmental narrative or dialogue-based storytelling means all manifestation is visual/behavioral through character design and presence/absence in successive titles.
